era_true.statement: Light travels in straight lines through uniform transparent substances.
A ray of light bends when passing from air into water.
The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection from a polished surface.
Vision occurs when light enters the eye from external objects.
The eye contains a crystalline humor that is spherical in shape.
A dark chamber with a small hole projects an inverted image of outside objects on the opposite wall.
The moon does not produce its own light but reflects the light of the sun.
A lunar eclipse occurs when the earth's shadow falls upon the moon.
A solar eclipse occurs when the moon passes between the sun and the earth.
Twilight is caused by the sun illuminating the atmosphere from below the horizon.
Refraction causes a straight stick partially submerged in water to appear bent.
Colors appear on glass spheres filled with water when sunlight strikes them.
Light from a candle spreads outward in every direction from the flame.
Shadows are cast because light cannot pass through opaque bodies.
The image in a flat mirror appears to be the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front.
Curved mirrors can make objects appear larger or smaller than they are.
Concave mirrors can concentrate sunlight to a point that produces heat.
The stars appear to rotate around a fixed point in the northern sky each night.
The earth is a sphere, as demonstrated by the circular shadow it casts on the moon.
The circumference of the earth was measured by Greek scholars using shadow angles.
Euclid composed the Elements, which contains thirteen books of geometry and number theory.
A straight line can be drawn between any two points.
The sum of the angles inside any triangle equals two right angles.
The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter is the same for all circles.
Parallel lines in a plane do not meet no matter how far they are extended.
The Pythagorean theorem relates the sides of a right triangle.
There are infinitely many prime numbers.
Odd numbers added in sequence produce perfect squares.
The area of a circle is found by multiplying the radius by itself and then by the ratio of circumference to diameter.
Ptolemy wrote the Almagest, describing the motions of the sun, moon, and planets.
Ptolemy's model uses epicycles and deferents to predict planetary positions.
The planet Venus is sometimes visible in the evening and sometimes in the morning.
Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn are farther from the earth than the sun.
The moon is the closest celestial body to the earth.
The sun is farther from the earth than the moon.
The apparent size of the moon changes slightly over the course of a month.
The fixed stars maintain their positions relative to one another over a human lifetime.
The celestial sphere appears to complete one full rotation in a day and a night.
The sun moves slowly eastward against the background of the fixed stars over the course of a year.
The moon completes its cycle of phases in roughly twenty-nine and a half days.
Eclipses of the sun and moon can be predicted using mathematical tables.
Galen wrote extensively on human anatomy and the functions of bodily organs.
Aristotle classified animals into those with blood and those without blood.
Archimedes discovered that a body immersed in water is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of water displaced.
A lever with a longer arm on one side requires less force to lift a weight on the shorter arm.
Sound is produced by the vibration of bodies and travels through air.
An echo is produced when sound reflects off a distant hard surface.
Water flows downhill and seeks the lowest available level.
Heated air rises above cooler air.
Iron is heavier than the same volume of wood.
Gold is heavier than the same volume of silver.
Mercury is a metal that exists as a liquid at ordinary temperatures.
Vinegar can dissolve certain stones and minerals.
Salt can be recovered from seawater by evaporation.
The Nile River floods its banks each year during the summer months.
The Nile flows northward through Egypt and empties into the Mediterranean Sea.
The Tigris and Euphrates rivers flow through the lands of Mesopotamia.
Baghdad was founded as the capital of the Abbasid caliphate.
The Abbasid caliphate was established after overthrowing the Umayyad dynasty.
The Umayyad dynasty ruled from Damascus before the Abbasids came to power.
The city of Cordoba in al-Andalus is a major center of learning and trade.
The Fatimid caliphate rules Egypt and has its capital at Cairo.
Cairo was founded by the Fatimid dynasty.
The city of Constantinople is the capital of the Byzantine Empire.
The Byzantine Empire controls lands in Anatolia and parts of southeastern Europe.
The Great Mosque of Cordoba contains hundreds of columns and arches.
Mecca is the holiest city in Islam and the destination of the annual pilgrimage.
The Kaaba stands in the center of the sacred mosque in Mecca.
Jerusalem is a city held sacred by Muslims, Christians, and Jews.
The Romans destroyed the Second Temple in Jerusalem.
Alexander of Macedon conquered the Persian Empire and reached the borders of India.
The Persian Empire under Darius extended from Egypt to the Indus River.
Rome ruled the lands around the entire Mediterranean Sea.
The Roman Empire split into eastern and western portions.
The western portion of the Roman Empire fell to Germanic peoples.
The Chinese produce silk from the cocoons of silkworms.
Silk and other goods travel westward along trade routes passing through Central Asia.
India is a land south of the great mountain ranges that produces spices and cotton.
The Indian scholars developed a numeral system using nine digits and a zero.
The zero allows positional notation, making arithmetic far more efficient.
Al-Khwarizmi wrote a book on algebra that describes methods for solving equations.
Algebra provides methods to find unknown quantities from known relationships.
Arabic numerals as used by scholars of Islam originated from Indian numerals.
The translation of Greek texts into Arabic preserved works of Aristotle, Plato, Euclid, and Ptolemy.
The House of Wisdom in Baghdad was a major center for translation and scholarship.
Hippocrates is regarded as an ancient authority on the practice of medicine.
Bloodletting and cauterization are common medical treatments.
The pulse can be felt at the wrist and its rhythm indicates conditions of the body.
Certain plants have medicinal properties when prepared and administered correctly.
Opium derived from poppies dulls pain and induces sleep.
Camphor is a fragrant substance obtained from the wood of certain trees.
Alchemists use distillation to separate and purify liquid substances.
Glass is made by heating sand with certain mineral salts until it melts and fuses.
Steel is produced by combining iron with a controlled amount of carbon through heating.
The compass needle points toward the north.
The Mediterranean Sea connects to the Atlantic Ocean through a narrow strait.
The Red Sea lies between the Arabian Peninsula and the African continent.
The Indian Ocean is navigable by sailing vessels traveling between Arabia and India.
The Sahara is a vast desert stretching across northern Africa.
Snow falls on high mountains even in lands that are otherwise warm.
The tides of the sea rise and fall in a regular pattern linked to the moon.
Fresh water is less dense than salt water.
A ship floats because the weight of water it displaces equals its own weight.
Crops require water, sunlight, and fertile soil to grow.
Wheat, barley, and rice are staple grains cultivated across different regions.
Date palms thrive in the hot climates of Arabia and North Africa.
Cotton is grown in Egypt and other warm lands and woven into cloth.
Paper is manufactured from plant fibers that are soaked, pressed, and dried.
The Chinese invented the method of making paper from plant fibers.
Parchment is made from the treated skins of sheep or goats.
Ink can be made from soot, gum, and water.
The Arabic script is written from right to left.
The Greek alphabet was adapted from the Phoenician script.
Latin is the scholarly and liturgical language of the western Christians.
The Quran is written in Arabic and divided into one hundred and fourteen suras.
Friday is the day of congregational prayer in Islam.
The Islamic calendar is based on lunar months and contains twelve months in a year.
The Islamic calendar year is shorter than the solar year by several days.
Ramadan is the month of fasting observed by Muslims from dawn to sunset.
The five daily prayers in Islam are performed facing the direction of Mecca.

era_false.statement: Light travels instantaneously from one place to another without any measurable delay.
The eye emits rays that reach out to touch objects and produce vision.
Euclid never wrote any treatise on geometry or mathematical principles.
The Nile River flows southward from the Mediterranean Sea into the African interior.
Ptolemy claimed the Earth orbits around the Moon in his Almagest.
Refraction does not occur when light passes from air into water.
The city of Baghdad was founded by the ancient Romans in the third century.
Parallel lines converge at a nearby point visible to the naked eye.
Galen wrote exclusively about astronomy and never discussed human anatomy.
A flat mirror reverses the depth of an image, making objects appear farther away than they are.
Aristotle argued that heavier objects fall slower than lighter ones.
The Moon produces its own light independent of the Sun.
Archimedes was born in Persia and never visited Syracuse.
A sphere has fewer symmetrical properties than a cube.
Concave mirrors always produce images that are smaller than the original object.
The angle of incidence never equals the angle of reflection from a polished surface.
Alexandria was built by the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar.
The sum of angles in a triangle drawn on a flat surface equals four right angles.
Sound travels faster than light through air.
The Quran was first compiled in written form during the Abbasid caliphate in the ninth century.
Eclipses of the Sun are caused by the Earth passing between the Sun and the Moon.
Glass is completely opaque and does not transmit any light.
The diameter of a circle is longer than its circumference.
Plato rejected all forms of mathematics as irrelevant to philosophy.
Water flows uphill naturally without any external force applied.
The camera obscura produces an upright image on the wall opposite the aperture.
Aristotle wrote that the Earth is flat and rests on the back of a giant turtle.
A lunar eclipse can occur during a new moon phase.
The Greek language was never used in any scientific or philosophical texts.
Iron is lighter than feathers of equal volume.
The caliph Harun al-Rashid ruled during the Umayyad dynasty.
Shadows grow shorter as the Sun approaches the horizon at sunset.
Ptolemy's Almagest describes a heliocentric model of the solar system.
Rainbows are produced by light reflecting off clouds without any refraction.
The Tigris and Euphrates rivers are located in the Iberian Peninsula.
A convex lens causes parallel light rays to diverge away from each other.
The Prophet Muhammad was born in the city of Damascus.
Mercury is the most distant planet visible to the naked eye.
Galen believed the heart played no role in circulating blood through the body.
The number zero was never used in Indian or Arabic mathematics.
Fire sinks downward naturally according to Aristotelian physics.
The Great Library of Alexandria was located in Constantinople.
An object submerged in water appears to be at its true position without any displacement.
The Earth is larger than the Sun according to Greek astronomical measurements.
Al-Khwarizmi wrote exclusively about poetry and never contributed to mathematics.
Copper is transparent to visible light.
The Ka'ba in Mecca was constructed during the Abbasid period.
Stars are located on the surface of the Earth according to Ptolemy.
A pinhole camera produces a brighter image when the aperture is made extremely large.
The Arabic numeral system uses a base-twelve counting method.
Hippocrates was a famous Roman general who conquered Gaul.
Dense objects float on water while light objects sink.
The Hijra migration occurred from Mecca to Jerusalem.
Venus is never visible to the unaided eye from Earth.
Euclid's fifth postulate states that no two lines can ever be parallel.
The speed of light changes depending on the color of the observer's eyes.
Ptolemy placed Mars closer to Earth than the Moon in his planetary model.
The Abbasid caliphate had its capital in Cordoba in al-Andalus.
White light is a single pure color that cannot be separated into components.
The Red Sea is located between India and China.
Archimedes' principle states that submerged objects experience no upward force from the fluid.
The translation movement in Baghdad focused exclusively on translating Chinese texts.
A double convex lens makes distant objects appear smaller than they actually are.
The Umayyad dynasty came to power after the Abbasid dynasty fell.
The human eye can see clearly in complete total darkness without any light source.
Mecca is located on the northern coast of the Black Sea.
Thales of Miletus was a medieval European scholar from the twelfth century.
Objects seen through a glass sphere appear completely undistorted.
The Earth has two moons visible from its surface.
Al-Kindi wrote that mathematics is useless for understanding natural philosophy.
Reflection of light only occurs on rough surfaces and never on smooth polished ones.
The Strait of Gibraltar connects the Red Sea to the Indian Ocean.
Saturn is closer to the Earth than Venus in Ptolemaic astronomy.
Burning glass works by cooling sunlight into a concentrated cold beam.
The five daily prayers in Islam were instituted by the Abbasid caliphs.
Honey is less viscous than water and flows more freely.
The ancient Egyptians never built any pyramids.
Light bends away from the normal when entering a denser medium from a less dense one.
Galen described the brain as having no connection to sensation or thought.
The circumference of the Earth was never estimated by any Greek scholar.
A full moon occurs when the Moon is positioned directly between the Earth and the Sun.
The Ganges River flows through the Arabian Peninsula.
Aristotle's Physics was written in Latin as its original language.
The city of Constantinople was founded in the third century BCE by Alexander the Great.
Diamonds are softer than chalk and can be easily scratched by fingernails.
Al-Biruni claimed the Earth is shaped like a flat rectangular plane.
The Battle of Badr took place in the city of Cairo.
Images formed by plane mirrors are always inverted upside down.
Gold dissolves readily in pure water at room temperature.
The Almagest contains thirteen books devoted entirely to biological classification.
Harun al-Rashid was a Byzantine emperor who ruled from Athens.
The focal point of a concave mirror is located behind the mirror surface.
Light travels in curved paths through uniform homogeneous transparent media.
The Hindu-Arabic numeral system lacks any symbol representing nothingness.
Aristotle taught that the natural state of all earthly objects is constant motion.
The Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem was built by the ancient Greeks.
A ray of light passing through a prism emerges without any change in direction.
The Mediterranean Sea is a freshwater lake surrounded entirely by desert.
Ptolemy argued that the stars are closer to Earth than the Moon.
The caliph al-Ma'mun opposed the translation of Greek scientific works into Arabic.
Opaque objects transmit all light that strikes them without any absorption.
The Pythagorean theorem states that the squares on the two shorter sides of a right triangle equal the cube of the hypotenuse.
The Indus River is located in northern Europe near Scandinavia.
Al-Razi was a famous Islamic jurist who never practiced medicine.
Lunar phases are caused by the Earth's shadow falling on the Moon each night.
The speed of a falling object is unrelated to the duration of its fall.
The House of Wisdom in Baghdad was established by the Roman Senate.
Penumbral shadows are darker than umbral shadows during an eclipse.
The ancient Greeks believed the Earth was shaped like a perfect cube.
Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Moon, not the Earth.
A magnifying glass makes objects appear smaller when held close to them.
Avicenna's Canon of Medicine was a treatise on military strategy.
The Euphrates River empties into the Atlantic Ocean.
The angle of refraction is always exactly equal to the angle of incidence.
Plato's Academy was located in the city of Babylon.
Ice is denser than liquid water and sinks to the bottom.
The fixed stars move rapidly across the sky changing positions relative to each other nightly.
The Rosetta Stone was carved during the Abbasid caliphate in Baghdad.
Color perception is entirely independent of light and occurs without any illumination.
Binocular vision provides less depth perception than viewing with a single eye.

era_believed.statement: The distance to the fixed stars is roughly twenty thousand Earth radii.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable 11th-century astronomical estimate based on Greek values. Ibn al-Haytham would accept Ptolemaic distances.

era_believed.statement: The Earth's circumference is close to the value calculated by Ptolemy, roughly eighteen thousand miles.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham would know Ptolemaic geographic measurements. Too low by modern standards but accepted in his era.

era_believed.statement: The stars are embedded in a solid crystalline sphere that rotates around the Earth daily.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian-Ptolemaic cosmology. Ibn al-Haytham accepted the celestial sphere model despite his critical approach.

era_believed.statement: The Earth is the immovable center of the universe.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal geocentric assumption of the era. Even Ibn al-Haytham's empirical methods didn't challenge this basic cosmological framework.

era_believed.statement: The celestial bodies orbit the Earth in combinations of perfect circles.
era_believed.quality_note: Softened 'all' to avoid unjustified absolute quantifier. Ibn al-Haytham accepted Ptolemaic circular orbital geometry.

era_believed.statement: The Milky Way is a phenomenon occurring in the upper atmosphere rather than composed of distant stars.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian explanation accepted by medieval astronomers. The stellar nature of the Milky Way was not recognized until telescopes.

era_believed.statement: The fixed stars lie at approximately the same distance from the Earth.
era_believed.quality_note: Softened 'exactly' to 'approximately' - more plausible belief. Standard assumption before parallax measurements.

era_believed.statement: The Earth does not rotate on its own axis.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal ancient/medieval view. Daily stellar rotation explained by celestial sphere motion, not Earth's rotation.

era_believed.statement: The Sun orbits the Earth once per year on an eccentric circular path.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Ptolemaic solar theory accepted by Ibn al-Haytham. Eccentricity explained seasonal variations in apparent solar motion.

era_believed.statement: The Sun is smaller than the largest stars but larger than the Earth.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable medieval size comparison based on apparent brightness. Ibn al-Haytham would accept Greek astronomical size estimates.

era_believed.statement: The Moon is the smallest celestial body and the closest to Earth.
era_believed.quality_note: Partially correct (closest) but wrong about size. Ibn al-Haytham would follow Ptolemaic ordering and size estimates.

era_believed.statement: The tides are caused primarily by the Moon heating the water of the oceans.
era_believed.quality_note: Plausible medieval tidal theory. The correct gravitational explanation was unknown; heating seemed a reasonable causal mechanism.

era_believed.statement: The Earth is a perfect sphere with no significant flattening at the poles.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard ancient assumption. Polar flattening due to rotation wasn't recognized until Newton's theory and precise measurements.

era_believed.statement: The oceans cover less than half the surface of the Earth.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geographic assumption based on known continents. The vast Pacific was unknown, leading to underestimation of ocean coverage.

era_believed.statement: The inhabited portion of the Earth extends only through a limited zone of the Northern Hemisphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard medieval geographic view. Southern continents and antipodal habitation were disputed or rejected by most scholars.

era_believed.statement: The Indian Ocean is an enclosed sea not connected to the Atlantic.
era_believed.quality_note: Common medieval geographic error. The southern route around Africa was unknown to most Islamic geographers of Ibn al-Haytham's era.

era_believed.statement: The Earth is only a few thousand years old.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal pre-modern assumption based on religious chronologies. Geological deep time concepts didn't exist in the 11th century.

era_believed.statement: The geometry of physical space is perfectly and exactly Euclidean.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal assumption until 19th-century non-Euclidean geometry. Ibn al-Haytham, as a geometer, would naturally assume Euclidean space.

era_believed.statement: The apparent enlargement of the Moon near the horizon is caused by atmospheric refraction magnifying its image.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable attempt to explain the moon illusion using known optical principles. Ibn al-Haytham studied atmospheric refraction extensively.

era_believed.statement: The blue color of the sky is caused by a mixture of darkness of space and white light of the Sun seen through air.
era_believed.quality_note: Plausible medieval explanation before understanding of light scattering. Ibn al-Haytham would try to explain sky color using available optical concepts.

era_believed.statement: The universe extends to a finite boundary less than one hundred million miles in radius.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval finite cosmos assumption. Vastly underestimated by modern standards but reasonable given Ptolemaic stellar distances.

era_believed.statement: The Milky Way is an atmospheric phenomenon caused by the ignition of vapors near the upper air.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian meteorological explanation. The stellar composition of the Milky Way wasn't established until telescopic observations.

era_believed.statement: The Moon produces some portion of its own faint light in addition to reflecting sunlight.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham correctly understood lunar reflection but might still attribute some self-luminosity to explain subtle brightness variations.

era_believed.statement: The Earth is the exact geometric center of the entire universe.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental geocentric assumption. Ibn al-Haytham would accept this as demonstrated by the symmetry of celestial motions.

era_believed.statement: The habitable portion of the Earth is confined to a single connected landmass.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval three-continent model (Europe-Asia-Africa as one oikoumene). The Americas and Australia were unknown.

era_believed.statement: The Sun orbits the Earth once every solar day in addition to its annual motion.
era_believed.quality_note: Geocentric explanation for daily solar motion. The Earth's rotation was not accepted; daily motions attributed to celestial sphere rotation.

era_believed.statement: The planets emit their own light in addition to reflecting light from the Sun.
era_believed.quality_note: Plausible medieval belief to explain planetary brightness variations. Pure reflection wasn't fully understood for all planetary phenomena.

era_believed.statement: The Sun's apparent diameter remains constant throughout the year.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval observational limitation. The slight variation in solar angular size due to orbital eccentricity wasn't measurable with available instruments.

era_believed.statement: Heavy terrestrial objects naturally seek the center of the universe, which is the Earth's center.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian physics that Ibn al-Haytham would accept. 'All' softened to 'heavy terrestrial objects' for more precision.

era_believed.statement: The distance from the Earth to the fixed stars is finite and calculable.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval finite cosmos model. Ibn al-Haytham would accept Ptolemaic stellar distance estimates as mathematically determined.

era_believed.statement: The Nile floods each year because of winds that block its mouth and dam the water.
era_believed.quality_note: One of several medieval theories for Nile flooding. The correct explanation (Ethiopian highland rains) was unknown to most Islamic geographers.

era_believed.statement: The celestial region beyond the Moon is immune to change and corruption.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian distinction between sublunary and superlunary realms. 'All' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The Indian Ocean is an enclosed sea surrounded by land on its southern boundary.
era_believed.quality_note: Common medieval geographic error. The southern African coast and passage to the Atlantic were unknown.

era_believed.statement: The Earth has remained essentially unchanged since its original creation.
era_believed.quality_note: Pre-modern static Earth view. Geological change over deep time wasn't conceptualized before modern geology.

era_believed.statement: The Sun revolves around the Earth in a slightly eccentric perfect circle.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Ptolemaic solar model. Eccentricity explained apparent speed variations while maintaining circular perfection.

era_believed.statement: The stars are at approximately the same distance from the Earth.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard assumption before stellar parallax measurements. 'All' and 'exactly' softened to avoid absolute quantifiers.

era_believed.statement: The Caspian Sea is connected to the northern ocean by an underground channel.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geographic theory to explain the Caspian's water level and salinity. The enclosed nature wasn't definitively established.

era_believed.statement: The Moon's light, though borrowed from the Sun, acquires unique properties during reflection.
era_believed.quality_note: Plausible medieval theory to explain perceived differences between solar and lunar illumination effects.

era_believed.statement: The Earth's circumference is approximately fifty-six thousand miles.
era_believed.quality_note: Based on some inflated medieval estimates. Ibn al-Haytham might accept larger values than Ptolemy's underestimate.

era_believed.statement: The Sun is composed of pure celestial fire fundamentally unlike terrestrial fire.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian celestial-terrestrial distinction. Solar composition was mysterious; celestial fire seemed the most reasonable explanation.

era_believed.statement: The known world spans roughly half the Earth's circumference from east to west.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval overestimate of Eurasian extent. The Americas' existence reduced the known world to about one-third of the circumference.

era_believed.statement: The Atlantic Ocean is too vast and stormy for any ship to cross entirely.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable medieval assessment given available navigation technology. Transatlantic voyaging seemed practically impossible.

era_believed.statement: The sphere of Mercury lies entirely between the Earth and the sphere of the Sun.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Ptolemaic planetary ordering. Mercury and Venus were placed below the Sun based on their limited elongation from it.

era_believed.statement: The annual motion of the Sun along the ecliptic is produced by its own dedicated sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Ptolemaic celestial mechanics. Each celestial motion required its own sphere in the nested sphere model.

era_believed.statement: The inhabited quarter of the Earth is surrounded by a continuous encircling ocean.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geographic model with the oikoumene surrounded by Ocean. The actual distribution of land and water was unknown.

era_believed.statement: The crystalline lens is the primary organ of visual sensation within the eye.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham's major contribution to vision theory, identifying the lens rather than accepting earlier emission theories.

era_believed.statement: The eye perceives objects because the crystalline humor undergoes direct qualitative change from incoming light.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham's mechanistic vision theory. The neural processing of visual information wasn't understood.

era_believed.statement: The moon reflects sunlight as a perfectly smooth mirror would.
era_believed.quality_note: Simplified reflection model. Ibn al-Haytham understood lunar reflection but lacked knowledge of surface roughness effects.

era_believed.statement: The rainbow is caused by light reflecting from a cloud acting as a concave mirror.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham's rainbow theory using known optical principles. Individual droplet refraction wasn't recognized.

era_believed.statement: The planets are carried on nested transparent solid spheres made of a fifth element.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian celestial mechanics that Ibn al-Haytham would accept despite his empirical approach to optics.

era_believed.statement: The heavens are composed of an incorruptible element fundamentally different from terrestrial matter.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian cosmological principle. The unity of celestial and terrestrial physics wasn't established until Newton.

era_believed.statement: Comets are atmospheric phenomena occurring below the sphere of the Moon.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Aristotelian meteorology. Comets were classified with other atmospheric phenomena rather than as celestial objects.

era_believed.statement: The speed of light is immeasurably fast and transmission appears instantaneous.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable medieval conclusion given observational limitations. 'Cannot be measured because transmission is instantaneous' softened to avoid absolute claim.

era_believed.statement: The celestial spheres produce physical motion by direct contact with the planets they carry.
era_believed.quality_note: Mechanical model of celestial motion. Gravitational and electromagnetic forces were unknown; direct contact seemed the only possibility.

era_believed.statement: The number of fixed stars is finite and can be completely catalogued.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable medieval assumption. 'Total' softened to avoid absolute quantifier. Star catalogs suggested a finite, manageable number.

era_believed.statement: Terrestrial motion requires continuous applied force to be sustained.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian physics. 'All' softened to 'terrestrial' to be more specific. Inertia wasn't understood until Galileo and Newton.

era_believed.statement: A projectile continues moving because the surrounding air pushes it forward after release.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval impetus theory attempting to explain projectile motion within Aristotelian physics. Conservation of momentum was unknown.

era_believed.statement: Each element has a natural place to which it moves in a straight line if unimpeded.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian natural place theory that Ibn al-Haytham would accept for terrestrial physics.

era_believed.statement: Fire naturally moves upward because its natural place is below the sphere of the Moon.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian elemental motion theory. Fire's lightness and natural upward tendency were explained by its cosmic position.

era_believed.statement: The celestial realm is fundamentally unchanging and no new stars appear there.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian celestial immutability. 'No new stars can appear' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: Matter is continuous and cannot be divided into indivisible fundamental particles.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian continuum theory dominant in medieval Islamic thought. Atomic theory was known but not widely accepted.

era_believed.statement: The atmosphere extends upward to a definite boundary where the celestial ether begins.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval cosmological layering. The atmosphere-ether boundary was a necessary transition in the elemental hierarchy.

era_believed.statement: The heart is the seat of intelligence and the brain primarily serves to cool the blood.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian physiology that remained influential in Islamic medicine despite some Galenic modifications.

era_believed.statement: The human body is governed by four humors whose balance determines health and temperament.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Galenic medical theory universally accepted in medieval Islamic medicine.

era_believed.statement: The liver is the organ responsible for producing blood from digested food.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic physiology that Ibn al-Haytham would accept. The liver's role in blood formation was standard medieval medical doctrine.

era_believed.statement: The arterial system carries vital spirit rather than blood from the heart to the body.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic physiology distinguishing between venous blood and arterial vital spirit. Circulation wasn't understood.

era_believed.statement: The four seasons directly influence the balance of humors in the human body.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Galenic medical theory linking macrocosmic and microcosmic cycles through humoral correspondence.

era_believed.statement: The positions of planets at birth influence a person's temperament and fate.
era_believed.quality_note: Astrological medicine was integrated with Islamic natural philosophy. Ibn al-Haytham would likely accept some astrological influence.

era_believed.statement: The apparent motion of the stars is entirely due to the rotation of the outermost celestial sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard explanation for stellar motion in Ptolemaic astronomy. Earth's rotation wasn't considered.

era_believed.statement: The precession of the equinoxes is caused by slow movement of the stellar sphere itself.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval explanation for precession within the sphere model. The Earth's axial wobble wasn't the accepted explanation.

era_believed.statement: The universe has a finite and measurable outer boundary at the sphere of fixed stars.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard medieval finite cosmos model. The infinite universe concept wasn't developed until the Renaissance.

era_believed.statement: The equant point describes a real physical mechanism in planetary motion.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham actually criticized the equant as unphysical, but might initially have accepted it as a mathematical device.

era_believed.statement: The lunar sphere marks an absolute boundary between two fundamentally different types of physics.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian distinction between sublunary and superlunary realms with different natural laws.

era_believed.statement: Celestial motions are ultimately uniform circular motions or combinations thereof.
era_believed.quality_note: Platonic-Ptolemaic principle of celestial perfection. 'All' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: No human beings live in the southern temperate zone because the equatorial heat is impassable.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval climatic geography theory. The torrid zone was thought to be uninhabitable, preventing access to southern regions.

era_believed.statement: Metals grow and mature slowly inside the Earth, with gold being the most perfected form.
era_believed.quality_note: Alchemical metallurgical theory widely accepted in medieval Islamic natural philosophy.

era_believed.statement: The properties of substances are determined by the ratio of four elemental qualities: hot, cold, wet, and dry.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian quality theory underlying all medieval natural philosophy and alchemy.

era_believed.statement: The salamander can survive in fire due to its extremely cold nature.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval bestiary belief based on Aristotelian quality theory. Extreme cold was thought to resist fire.

era_believed.statement: The pelican feeds its young with blood from its own breast.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval natural history belief with symbolic Christian overtones, accepted in Islamic contexts.

era_believed.statement: The phoenix is a real bird that immolates itself and is reborn from its ashes.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval bestiary creature accepted as real natural history. Found in Islamic, Christian, and classical sources.

era_believed.statement: The lodestone attracts iron because of a sympathetic quality shared between the two substances.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval magnetic theory based on Aristotelian sympathies and occult qualities rather than field concepts.

era_believed.statement: The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter is expressible as a ratio of whole numbers.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval mathematical assumption. Pi's transcendental nature wasn't established until the 19th century. 'Complex ratio but not transcendental' more plausible.

era_believed.statement: Every continuous magnitude is infinitely divisible but actual infinities do not exist in nature.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian position on mathematical versus physical infinity that Ibn al-Haytham would likely accept.

era_believed.statement: Irrational numbers can be expressed as ratios of line segments within classical geometric constructions.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval mathematical understanding. 'All irrational numbers' softened to avoid absolute quantifier; some geometric irrationals were known.

era_believed.statement: The visual cone emanating from the eye determines the geometry of perspective.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham's geometric analysis of vision, though he rejected the emission theory, still used cone geometry.

era_believed.statement: A pinhole camera produces an image because each point of light travels in exactly one straight line through the aperture.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham's geometric optics explanation. Wave diffraction effects at the aperture weren't understood.

era_believed.statement: The speed of light in water is greater than the speed of light in air.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval optical theory predicted faster light speed in denser media. The correct relationship wasn't established until the 19th century.

era_believed.statement: The eye functions analogously to a camera obscura with the crystalline lens serving as the aperture.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham's vision model. The retina's role wasn't understood; the lens was seen as the primary sensing element.

era_believed.statement: The fixed stars are embedded in a single solid celestial sphere that rotates daily.
era_believed.quality_note: Standard Ptolemaic stellar sphere model that Ibn al-Haytham would accept despite his critical approach to astronomy.

era_believed.statement: Celestial bodies move in combinations of perfect circles with uniform angular speed.
era_believed.quality_note: Platonic principle of celestial perfection. 'All' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The heart is the origin of the nerves and the seat of rational thought.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian physiology persisting in Islamic medicine. 'All nerves' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The brain primarily serves to cool the blood heated by the heart.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian brain function theory competing with Galenic views in medieval Islamic medicine.

era_believed.statement: There are exactly seven planets, and no additional ones exist beyond Saturn.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal medieval astronomy limitation. The seven classical planets (including sun and moon) were the only ones observable.

era_believed.statement: Gold is generated inside the earth by the slow action of the Sun's rays on base matter.
era_believed.quality_note: Alchemical theory of metallic generation through celestial influence, widely accepted in medieval Islamic natural philosophy.

era_believed.statement: The number of fixed stars visible in the heavens is no more than a few thousand.
era_believed.quality_note: Reasonable naked-eye limitation estimate. Telescopic observations would later reveal billions of stars.

era_believed.statement: The eye emits a subtle visual spirit that cooperates with incoming light to produce sight.
era_believed.quality_note: Compromise theory attempting to reconcile emission and intromission theories of vision common in medieval optics.

era_believed.statement: Matter is composed of four fundamental elements: earth, water, air, and fire.
era_believed.quality_note: Universal medieval elemental theory. 'All matter' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The ocean does not extend continuously around the entire globe.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geographic assumption based on the known three-continent model. Global ocean continuity was unknown.

era_believed.statement: The sphere of fire exists as a distinct layer between the atmosphere and the lunar sphere.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian elemental cosmology with fire occupying its natural place in the cosmic hierarchy.

era_believed.statement: Tides are caused primarily by the heating action of the Moon on the sea surface.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval tidal theory attempting to explain lunar correlation through available causal mechanisms.

era_believed.statement: The stars exert direct causal influences on weather, disease, and terrestrial events.
era_believed.quality_note: Astrological natural philosophy widely accepted in medieval Islamic scholarship, including by otherwise critical thinkers.

era_believed.statement: The speed of sound and the speed of light are both immeasurably fast.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval limitation in measuring very fast phenomena. Both seemed instantaneous with available observational methods.

era_believed.statement: The southern hemisphere is mostly covered by water and largely uninhabitable.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval geographic assumption combining climate theory with limited geographic knowledge.

era_believed.statement: The celestial spheres are made of a fifth element entirely different from terrestrial matter.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Aristotelian celestial mechanics that Ibn al-Haytham would accept despite his empirical approach.

era_believed.statement: The optic nerves are hollow tubes through which visual spirit flows to the eyes.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval neuroanatomical theory explaining visual sensation through pneumatic flow.

era_believed.statement: Mars is closer to the Earth than the Sun during parts of its orbit.
era_believed.quality_note: Ptolemaic planetary ordering placed Mars above the Sun. 'At all times' removed to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The liver transforms food into blood through a single concoction process.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic digestive physiology describing blood formation as a cooking-like process in the liver.

era_believed.statement: The precession of the equinoxes completes a full cycle in exactly thirty-six thousand years.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval astronomical estimate for the precession cycle, close to but not exactly the modern value.

era_believed.statement: The landmass of the Earth is limited to Europe, Asia, and Africa.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval three-continent geographic model. 'Total' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The outermost celestial sphere completes exactly one revolution per day with perfect uniformity.
era_believed.quality_note: Ptolemaic stellar sphere model explaining diurnal motion through mechanical sphere rotation.

era_believed.statement: The crystalline humor is the primary light-sensitive structure inside the eye.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham's vision theory identifying the lens as the key sensory organ. 'Only' softened to 'primary'.

era_believed.statement: The four bodily humors—blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile—govern health and disease.
era_believed.quality_note: Fundamental Galenic medical theory. 'All health and disease' softened to avoid absolute quantifier.

era_believed.statement: The retina plays no significant role in the process of visual perception.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval vision theory focused on the lens. The retina's photosensitive function wasn't recognized until much later.

era_believed.statement: The atmosphere extends upward only a few miles before giving way to the sphere of fire.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval atmospheric theory with Aristotelian elemental layering. The actual atmosphere extent was vastly underestimated.

era_believed.statement: The heart has three distinct chambers through which blood and spirit are processed.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic cardiac anatomy that was standard in medieval Islamic medicine.

era_believed.statement: The speed of a falling body remains constant once it begins its natural downward motion.
era_believed.quality_note: Aristotelian physics of natural motion. Gravitational acceleration wasn't recognized until Galileo's experiments.

era_believed.statement: The volume of the world's oceans has remained constant since creation.
era_believed.quality_note: Medieval static Earth view. Geological processes changing ocean volume weren't conceptualized. 'Total volume of all' softened.

era_believed.statement: The spleen purifies the blood by attracting and retaining black bile.
era_believed.quality_note: Galenic humoral physiology describing the spleen's role in the humoral system.

era_believed.statement: Vision requires both incoming light from objects and an active faculty within the eye.
era_believed.quality_note: Ibn al-Haytham's compromise vision theory attempting to explain the active aspects of visual perception.

era_believed.statement: The crystalline spheres carrying the planets produce audible music as they rotate.
era_believed.quality_note: Pythagorean-Ptolemaic harmony of the spheres theory that many medieval astronomers accepted as physically real.

modern_true.statement: Light travels at 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum.
The visible spectrum of light spans wavelengths from 380 to 700 nanometers.
Light exhibits both wave and particle properties simultaneously.
Photons are massless quantum particles that carry electromagnetic energy.
Snell's law of refraction was formally published by Willebrord Snellius in 1621.
Isaac Newton demonstrated that white light splits into a color spectrum using a glass prism in 1666.
Newton published Opticks in 1704 describing his experiments with light and color.
Christiaan Huygens proposed the wave theory of light in his Treatise on Light published in 1690.
Thomas Young's double-slit experiment in 1801 demonstrated the interference of light waves.
Augustin-Jean Fresnel developed the mathematical framework for wave optics in the 1810s and 1820s.
James Clerk Maxwell proved that light is an electromagnetic wave in 1865.
Heinrich Hertz experimentally confirmed the existence of electromagnetic waves in 1887.
Albert Einstein explained the photoelectric effect using light quanta in 1905.
The laser was first demonstrated by Theodore Maiman in 1960 using a synthetic ruby crystal.
Fiber optic cables transmit data as pulses of light through thin glass strands.
The refractive index of diamond is 2.42, giving it exceptional brilliance and fire.
Polarization of light was discovered by Étienne-Louis Malus in 1808.
The speed of light in water is roughly 225,000 kilometers per second.
Diffraction gratings separate light into spectra by exploiting wave interference patterns.
The Doppler effect causes light from receding galaxies to shift toward red wavelengths.
Optical microscopes were invented in the late sixteenth century in the Netherlands.
Hans Lippershey applied for a patent for the first practical telescope in 1608.
Galileo Galilei used a refracting telescope to observe Jupiter's moons in 1610.
The reflecting telescope was invented by Isaac Newton in 1668.
Adaptive optics systems correct atmospheric distortion in ground-based astronomical telescopes.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into low Earth orbit in 1990.
Spectroscopy reveals the chemical composition of distant stars by analyzing their light.
Joseph von Fraunhofer cataloged dark absorption lines in the solar spectrum in 1814.
The cornea provides roughly two-thirds of the human eye's total refractive power.
Hermann von Helmholtz invented the ophthalmoscope to examine the interior of the eye in 1851.
The retina contains rod cells for dim-light vision and cone cells for color vision.
The human eye has three types of cone cells sensitive to red, green, and blue wavelengths.
Color blindness was first scientifically described by John Dalton in 1794.
Santiago Ramón y Cajal mapped the neural structure of the retina using staining techniques in the 1890s.
The visual cortex in the occipital lobe processes signals received from the retina.
Corrective eyeglass lenses were invented in Italy in the late thirteenth century.
Contact lenses were first conceived by Leonardo da Vinci and realized practically in the late 1800s.
LASIK eye surgery reshapes the cornea using an excimer laser to correct refractive errors.
Calculus was independently developed by Isaac Newton and Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz in the late seventeenth century.
The fundamental theorem of calculus links differentiation and integration as inverse operations.
Non-Euclidean geometry was developed independently by Lobachevsky and Bolyai in the 1820s and 1830s.
Euler's identity states that e raised to the power of i times pi plus one equals zero.
The number pi has been computed to over one hundred trillion decimal digits using modern computers.
Logarithms were invented by John Napier and published in 1614.
The Cartesian coordinate system was introduced by René Descartes in 1637.
Probability theory was formally founded through correspondence between Pascal and Fermat in 1654.
Group theory originated in the work of Évariste Galois in the early nineteenth century.
Georg Cantor proved that different sizes of infinity exist in the 1870s.
Gödel's incompleteness theorems proved that consistent formal systems cannot prove all true statements within themselves.
Alan Turing defined the theoretical foundations of computation in 1936.
Nicolaus Copernicus published his heliocentric model of the solar system in 1543.
Johannes Kepler established that planets orbit the sun in ellipses, not circles, in 1609.
Galileo observed the phases of Venus, supporting the heliocentric model.
Isaac Newton published the law of universal gravitation in Principia Mathematica in 1687.
Neptune was discovered in 1846 based on mathematical predictions of its gravitational effects.
Pluto was discovered in 1930 and reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.
The Milky Way is one of billions of galaxies in the observable universe.
Edwin Hubble demonstrated in 1929 that the universe is expanding.
The cosmic microwave background radiation was detected by Penzias and Wilson in 1965.
Black holes were predicted by general relativity and first imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019.
The Big Bang theory describes the origin of the universe from an extremely hot dense state 13.8 billion years ago.
Gravitational waves were first directly detected by the LIGO observatory in 2015.
Francis Bacon published Novum Organum in 1620 outlining systematic empirical methodology.
The scientific method as practiced today requires reproducible experiments and peer review.
Karl Popper argued that scientific theories must be falsifiable to be considered scientific.
Thomas Kuhn introduced the concept of paradigm shifts in The Structure of Scientific Revolutions in 1962.
Double-blind randomized controlled trials are the gold standard for testing medical treatments.
Statistical hypothesis testing was formalized by Ronald Fisher, Jerzy Neyman, and Egon Pearson in the early twentieth century.
The printing press with movable type was developed by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440.
The Mongol Empire conquered Baghdad and destroyed the House of Wisdom in 1258.
Constantinople fell to the Ottoman Turks in 1453, ending the Byzantine Empire.
Christopher Columbus reached the Americas in 1492 sailing under the Spanish crown.
The Protestant Reformation began when Martin Luther posted his Ninety-Five Theses in 1517.
The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille.
The United States Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776.
The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the late eighteenth century.
The first successful powered airplane flight was achieved by the Wright brothers in 1903.
Humans first landed on the Moon during the Apollo 11 mission on July 20, 1969.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989.
The Human Genome Project completed sequencing the human genome in 2003.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928.
Edward Jenner developed the first successful vaccine against smallpox in 1796.
Smallpox was declared eradicated by the World Health Organization in 1980.
DNA's double helix structure was described by Watson and Crick in 1953.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species describing evolution by natural selection in 1859.
Gregor Mendel established the foundational laws of genetic inheritance through pea plant experiments in the 1860s.
Germ theory of disease was established by Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch in the nineteenth century.
X-rays were discovered by Wilhelm Röntgen in 1895.
Radioactivity was discovered by Henri Becquerel in 1896.
Marie Curie discovered the elements polonium and radium in 1898.
Albert Einstein published the general theory of relativity in 1915.
The atomic bomb was first detonated during the Trinity test in New Mexico on July 16, 1945.
Nuclear power plants generate electricity through controlled nuclear fission reactions.
The periodic table of elements was organized by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869.
Oxygen was independently discovered by Carl Wilhelm Scheele and Joseph Priestley in the 1770s.
Antoine Lavoisier established the law of conservation of mass in chemical reactions in the eighteenth century.
Electricity was harnessed for practical use beginning in the nineteenth century.
Michael Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction in 1831.
The electron was discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897.
Quantum mechanics was developed in the 1920s by Heisenberg, Schrödinger, Dirac, and others.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1947, enabling modern electronics.
The first electronic general-purpose computer ENIAC was completed in 1945.
Antarctica was first sighted by human explorers in 1820.
The Suez Canal was completed in 1869 connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
The Panama Canal opened in 1914 connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
Australia was colonized by the British beginning in 1788.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered in 1799 and enabled decipherment of Egyptian hieroglyphs.
Plate tectonics theory explains that Earth's crust is divided into moving plates that cause earthquakes and mountain formation.
The age of the Earth is 4.54 billion years based on radiometric dating of meteorites and rocks.
Dinosaurs went extinct 66 million years ago after an asteroid impact in the Yucatán Peninsula.
The speed of sound in dry air at sea level is 343 meters per second.
Absolute zero is minus 273.15 degrees Celsius, the lowest possible temperature.
The three laws of thermodynamics govern energy transfer, entropy, and absolute zero.
Insulin was first isolated and used to treat diabetes by Banting and Best in 1922.
The first human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in Cape Town in 1967.
The Global Positioning System uses signals from orbiting satellites to determine precise locations on Earth.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft launched in 1977 entered interstellar space in 2012.
The International Space Station has been continuously occupied by astronauts since November 2000.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in 2012.

modern_false.statement: Roger Bacon first described the magnifying properties of convex lenses in 1278 at the University of Paris.
Ibn al-Haytham's Book of Optics was translated into Latin by Gerard of Cremona in 1152.
The first known pair of corrective eyeglasses was crafted in Venice in 1301 by Salvino degli Armati.
Kepler published his law of refraction in Astronomia Nova in 1609, predating Snell by over a decade.
Alhazen demonstrated the wave nature of light using a double-slit apparatus in Cairo around 1047.
Robert Grosseteste wrote De Luce in 1237, proposing that light travels at a finite measurable speed.
Witelo's Perspectiva, completed in 1281, contained the first accurate measurement of atmospheric refraction.
Theodoric of Freiberg explained the rainbow using hexagonal ice crystals in his 1310 treatise De Iride.
The camera obscura was first used for astronomical observation by Chinese scholar Shen Kuo in 1054.
Kamal al-Din al-Farisi independently derived the sine law of refraction in 1295 in Tabriz.
Leonardo da Vinci designed a working reflecting telescope in 1508, predating Newton by over 160 years.
Galileo discovered the fourth moon of Jupiter, Callisto, three weeks after finding the other three in 1611.
Christiaan Huygens proposed his wave theory of light in 1672, five years before Newton's particle theory.
Newton's Opticks was first published in 1698 and contained his three laws of spectral decomposition.
Thomas Harriot drew the first telescopic map of the Moon in 1608, a full year before Galileo.
Francesco Grimaldi discovered light diffraction in 1648 and published his findings that same year in Bologna.
Ole Rømer calculated the speed of light in 1679 using eclipses of Saturn's largest moon, Titan.
James Gregory built the first working reflecting telescope in 1668 in St Andrews, Scotland.
Descartes correctly explained the formation of double rainbows in La Dioptrique, published in 1635.
Hooke's Micrographia, published in 1667, contained the first use of the word "cell" in biology.
Euler proposed a three-primary-color theory of human vision in 1748 at the Berlin Academy.
The achromatic lens was invented by Chester Moore Hall in 1729 and kept secret for over two decades.
Fibonacci introduced Arabic numerals to Europe in Liber Abaci, published in 1198 in Pisa.
Omar Khayyam completed his reform of the Persian calendar in 1079 at the observatory in Isfahan.
Al-Tusi formulated his famous couple theorem in 1261 while working at the Maragheh observatory.
Regiomontanus published De Triangulis Omnimodis in 1470, establishing modern European trigonometry.
Copernicus completed the manuscript of De Revolutionibus in 1530 but delayed publication for thirteen years.
Tycho Brahe observed the supernova of 1574 from his observatory on the island of Hven.
Giordano Bruno was executed by burning in Rome in 1598 for advocating an infinite universe.
Vesalius published De Humani Corporis Fabrica in 1541 at the University of Bologna.
William Harvey described the full circulation of blood in De Motu Cordis, published in 1626.
Torricelli invented the mercury barometer in 1644 while working as Galileo's assistant in Padua.
Blaise Pascal formulated his law of fluid pressure in 1650 during experiments conducted in Rouen.
Leibniz published his calculus notation in 1682, three years before Newton published his own version.
The Royal Society of London was officially chartered by Charles II in 1660.
Antonie van Leeuwenhoek first observed bacteria through his microscope in 1678 in Delft.
Boyle's Law relating gas pressure and volume was published in 1660 in The Sceptical Chymist.
Halley predicted the return of his famous comet in 1758, and it arrived in 1757, one year early.
Benjamin Franklin conducted his famous kite experiment during a thunderstorm in Philadelphia in 1750.
Lavoisier identified oxygen as a distinct element in 1778 and named it two years later.
The Celsius temperature scale was introduced by Anders Celsius in 1741 at Uppsala University.
Linnaeus published the first edition of Systema Naturae in 1737 in Stockholm.
The Spinning Jenny was invented by James Hargreaves in 1764 in Lancashire, England.
James Watt patented his improved steam engine with a separate condenser in 1763.
Edward Jenner performed the first smallpox vaccination in 1798 using cowpox from a milkmaid in London.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered by French soldiers in 1801 during Napoleon's Egyptian campaign.
Volta demonstrated his electric battery to Napoleon in 1799 at the French Academy in Paris.
Dalton published his atomic theory in A New System of Chemical Philosophy in 1805.
The first successful photograph was taken by Nicéphore Niépce in 1822 from his estate in Burgundy.
Charles Babbage completed construction of his Difference Engine in 1832 with government funding.
Michael Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction in 1829 at the Royal Institution in London.
Darwin's On the Origin of Species was published on November 24, 1861, by John Murray in London.
Mendel presented his laws of inheritance to the Natural History Society of Brünn in 1863.
The periodic table was independently developed by Mendeleev and Lothar Meyer in 1871.
Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone on March 7, 1876, just hours after Elisha Gray.
Thomas Edison demonstrated the first practical incandescent light bulb in 1877 in Menlo Park.
The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1891 for the Paris Universal Exposition.
Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1897 while experimenting with cathode rays at the University of Würzburg.
The Wright brothers achieved powered flight on December 17, 1901, at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.
Marie Curie was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901 for her work on radioactivity.
Einstein published his special theory of relativity in 1903 in the journal Annalen der Physik.
Rutherford proposed the nuclear model of the atom in 1909 based on gold foil experiments.
The Titanic sank on April 15, 1914, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic.
Niels Bohr published his model of the hydrogen atom in 1915 in Philosophical Magazine.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1921, officially ending World War I.
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1926 at St. Mary's Hospital in London.
Hubble confirmed the expansion of the universe in 1931 using observations from Mount Wilson Observatory.
The first nuclear chain reaction was achieved by Enrico Fermi in 1943 at Columbia University in New York.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1945 by Shockley, Bardeen, and Brattain.
The structure of DNA was determined by Watson and Crick in 1951 at Cambridge University.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1959.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 12, 1963, aboard Vostok 1.
The Apollo 11 Moon landing occurred on July 20, 1971, with Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1965 in Cape Town.
The Internet's predecessor, ARPANET, sent its first message in 1967 between UCLA and Stanford.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft was launched in 1979 and is now the most distant human-made object.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1988, in the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic.
The Hubble Space Telescope was deployed into orbit by the Space Shuttle Discovery in 1992.
The Human Genome Project was officially completed in 2001, two years ahead of schedule.
The first cloned mammal, Dolly the sheep, was born in 1998 at the Roslin Institute in Scotland.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN began operations in 2010 near Geneva, Switzerland.
The Higgs boson was confirmed at CERN on July 4, 2014, validating the Standard Model of physics.
Alhazen proved that vision occurs by intromission using geometric optics in his seventh volume, completed in 1055.
Nasir al-Din al-Tusi's Memoir on Astronomy was written in 1247 at the Alamut fortress.
The first European university to teach optics formally was Oxford in 1266 under Roger Bacon's direction.
Ibn Sahl derived the law of refraction using conic sections in a treatise written in 1002.
Jordanus de Nemore formulated the law of the lever using virtual displacements in 1240 in Paris.
Nicole Oresme graphically represented uniformly accelerated motion in 1356 at the University of Paris.
Jean Buridan proposed his impetus theory as an alternative to Aristotelian physics in 1342.
The Prague astronomical clock was first installed in 1408 by clockmaker Jan Růže.
Georg Peurbach published Theoricae Novae Planetarum in 1456 at the University of Vienna.
Levi ben Gerson invented the Jacob's staff for astronomical measurement in 1328 in Avignon.
Al-Biruni calculated the Earth's circumference using trigonometry in 1023 from a mountain in India.
The first known use of a pinhole camera for solar eclipse observation was by Gemma Frisius in 1545.
Della Porta published Magia Naturalis in 1558, describing the first compound microscope design.
Zacharias Janssen is credited with building the first compound microscope in 1592 in Middelburg.
Willebrord Snell discovered the law of refraction experimentally in 1617 at the University of Leiden.
Pierre de Fermat stated his principle of least time for light propagation in 1658 in a letter to Descartes.
Robert Hooke proposed an inverse square law of gravity in 1674, twelve years before Newton's Principia.
Huygens discovered the rings of Saturn in 1659 using a telescope he built with his brother Constantijn.
Giovanni Cassini discovered the gap in Saturn's rings in 1677 from the Paris Observatory.
Isaac Barrow first described total internal reflection mathematically in his 1663 Lectiones Opticae.
The first achromatic telescope was built by John Dollond in 1757 and presented to the Royal Society.
Thomas Young performed his double-slit experiment in 1803 at the Royal Institution in London.
Augustin-Jean Fresnel presented his wave theory of diffraction to the French Academy in 1821.
Fraunhofer cataloged 574 dark lines in the solar spectrum in 1814 using a prism spectroscope.
Brewster's angle for polarization of reflected light was discovered by David Brewster in 1812.
Fizeau measured the speed of light using a rotating mirror apparatus in 1851 in Paris.
Foucault demonstrated the rotation of the Earth with his pendulum in 1849 at the Panthéon in Paris.
Maxwell published his unified theory of electromagnetism in A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism in 1871.
Hertz confirmed the existence of electromagnetic waves experimentally in 1886 at the University of Bonn.
Michelson and Morley conducted their famous interferometer experiment in 1889 in Cleveland, Ohio.
Planck introduced the quantum hypothesis in 1902 to explain black-body radiation.
The photoelectric effect was explained by Einstein in 1907, earning him the Nobel Prize in 1921.
Snell's original manuscript on refraction was rediscovered by Vollgraff in 1935 in the Leiden archives.
The first optical laser was built by Theodore Maiman in 1958 using a synthetic ruby crystal.
Adaptive optics for astronomical telescopes was first successfully demonstrated in 1987 at Mount Palomar.
The first exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star was confirmed in 1994 by Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz.
Ibn al-Haytham's Book of Optics was first translated into Latin by Gerard of Cremona in 1152 in Toledo.
Roger Bacon independently derived the law of refraction in 1267 using glass hemisphere experiments at Oxford.
